medications.indication_rationale: Naloxone is the specific antidote that reverses opioid-induced respiratory depression. In telehealth the AI Doctor must instruct the carer to give any available pre-dosed take-home naloxone device (intranasal spray or prefilled intramuscular auto-injector — designed for lay use, no dose calculation required) into the outer thigh or nostril, and repeat after ~3 minutes if there is no improvement, while 000 is called. The AI Doctor itself cannot administer parenteral naloxone.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Source clinician gave naloxone 400 microgram IM stat, repeated as needed. Community/telehealth pathway: a pre-dosed take-home naloxone device (e.g. intranasal 1.8 mg spray or prefilled IM auto-injector) given by the carer per device instructions, repeated after ~3 minutes if no response — titrated to restoring adequate breathing, not full arousal.
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Expected re-sedation ('renarcotisation'): naloxone's duration is shorter than long-acting opioids, so effect may wear off and coma recur — observation is mandatory.
Naloxone does NOT reverse the co-ingested benzodiazepine (diazepam); residual sedation should be expected and does not mean a second opioid cause.

medications.drug_name: supplemental oxygen and assisted ventilation (rescue breaths / BVM)
medications.indication_rationale: Correcting hypoxia is as important as the antidote. The carer can be coached to open the airway and give rescue breaths if he is not breathing; oxygen and bag-valve-mask ventilation are delivered by paramedics/ED — the AI Doctor coaches basic life support and escalates, it cannot deliver oxygen.

Flumazenil must NOT be used to reverse the benzodiazepine component in this mixed overdose with a chronic substance-use history — it can precipitate seizures and arrhythmia and unmask withdrawal. Supportive care and naloxone for the opioid component are correct; benzodiazepine effect is managed with observation and ventilatory support.
contraindications_in_this_case: Chronic sedative use (seizure risk on abrupt reversal)

medications.indication_rationale: Do NOT induce vomiting or give oral charcoal in a comatose patient with no airway protection — the aspiration risk is high and it is dangerous and contraindicated. Airway, breathing and naloxone come first.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Engage with community addiction/drug-and-alcohol services and consider ongoing opioid-agonist treatment after this admission.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Addresses the underlying opioid use disorder that drove this near-fatal event; hospital admission is a point of engagement.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Lock away all prescription medicines and keep a take-home naloxone kit available, with family trained to use it.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removes immediate access to lethal drugs during craving/crisis and puts a life-saving antidote within reach at the moment of exposure.

patient_education_points.point: This is a life-threatening emergency — his breathing had nearly stopped and he needs an ambulance and hospital care now.
Naloxone is temporary and wears off faster than the drugs he took, so the deep sleep and slow breathing can come back — he must be watched in hospital for many hours even if he improves.
Keep him on his side in the recovery position; do not try to make him drink or vomit, because he could choke.
Gather the empty packets and any remaining medicines to hand to the paramedics so the hospital knows exactly what he took.

follow_up_plan.interval: immediate ambulance transport now; then mandatory in-hospital observation for at least 12-24 hours
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: airway, breathing and consciousness; watch for re-sedation as naloxone wears off; screen for co-ingestants; then psychiatric and drug-and-alcohol review before discharge
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any slowing of breathing, deepening drowsiness or loss of consciousness as naloxone wears off — give a further naloxone dose and keep 000 on the line

must_include_items: Recognise a life-threatening opioid toxidrome (coma + slow breathing + pinpoint pupils) as a peri-arrest emergency
Call 000 for an emergency ambulance immediately, without delay
Instruct the carer to place him in the recovery position and open the airway, and give rescue breaths if he is not breathing
Instruct the carer to give any available pre-dosed take-home naloxone (intranasal/prefilled IM) and repeat after ~3 minutes if no response
Advise mandatory hospital observation because naloxone wears off before the opioid does (re-sedation risk)
acceptable_alternatives: If no naloxone kit is available at all, the emphasis shifts entirely to immediate 000, airway positioning and rescue breaths — the AI cannot supply naloxone.
errors_of_omission: Failing to give recovery-position / airway / rescue-breath coaching
Failing to advise take-home naloxone where available
Failing to warn about re-sedation and the need for hospital observation
errors_of_commission: Advising the carer to induce vomiting or give anything by mouth to an unconscious patient
Recommending flumazenil for the benzodiazepine component
Delaying naloxone/000 to identify the exact drug first
Under-triaging to GP, watch-and-wait, or self-managing at home
Being falsely reassured by transient improvement and standing down the ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: AUTOMATIC FAIL if the response does not call 000 (T5) OR fails to give airway/recovery-position coaching. Recommending flumazenil or inducing vomiting is a critical error of commission.
